channel.title: NPR Topics: Health
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: In his book, self-described USAID 'whistleblower' talks about the agency and Ebola
item.description: Nicholas Enrich, on staff at the U.S. Agency for International Aid under 4 administrations, talks about Into the Woodchipper: A Whistleblower's Account of How the Trump Administration Shredded USAID.
item.content:encoded:
'/>

Nicholas Enrich, on staff at the U.S. Agency for International Aid under 4 administrations, talks about Into the Woodchipper: A Whistleblower's Account of How the Trump Administration Shredded USAID.

(Image credit: Simon & Schuster)
item.dc:creator: Fatma Tanis

item.title: Can a vibrating belt help protect bones and muscle health?
item.description: More than 40 million adults in the U.S. ages 50 and older have osteopenia, or low bone density. An FDA-approved wearable vibration device is giving some women a tool that could slow that loss.
item.content:encoded:
More than 40 million adults in the U.S. ages 50 and older have osteopenia, or low bone density. An FDA-approved wearable vibration device is giving some women a tool that could slow that loss.

(Image credit: Maria Fabrizio for NPR)
item.dc:creator: Allison Aubrey

item.title: People love working from home. But does it love them back? A new study says no
item.description: A study finds that people in remote jobs are more socially isolated, anxious and sad compared to people not in remote jobs. But demanding everyone return to the office isn't the answer either, say researchers.
item.content:encoded:
A study finds that people in remote jobs are more socially isolated, anxious and sad compared to people not in remote jobs. But demanding everyone return to the office isn't the answer either, say researchers.

(Image credit: Lea Suzuki)
item.dc:creator: Rhitu Chatterjee

item.title: Ebola cases rise in Congo, as government revives travel restrictions
item.description: The Democratic Republic of Congo's Ebola outbreak is spreading at an unprecedented pace, Africa CDC warns.
item.content:encoded:
The Democratic Republic of Congo's Ebola outbreak is spreading at an unprecedented pace, Africa CDC warns.

(Image credit: Jospin Mwisha)
item.dc:creator: Emmet Livingstone

item.title: Texas has detected a flesh-eating parasite in livestock after 60 years
item.description: Texas officials announced they have detected the New World screwworm in livestock, the first evidence of the parasite in that state in decades.
item.content:encoded: Texas officials announced they have detected the New World screwworm in livestock, the first evidence of the parasite in that state in decades.
item.dc:creator: Will Stone

item.title: It's one of the world's most isolated islands. Here come the bulldozers
item.description: The Indian government is spending $9 billion to create a megaport, airport and city on this remote island. Critics fear the impact on pristine forests and the lives of indigenous inhabitants.
item.content:encoded:
The Indian government is spending $9 billion to create a megaport, airport and city on this remote island. Critics fear the impact on pristine forests and the lives of indigenous inhabitants.

(Image credit: Omkar Khandekar/NPR)
item.dc:creator: Omkar Khandekar

item.title: When U.S. foreign aid changed, AIDS workers in Africa felt it
item.description: In South Africa and Mozambique, health care providers say cancellation or redirection of U.S. PEPFAR funding under the Trump administration have already endangered vulnerable people and cost lives.
item.content:encoded:
In South Africa and Mozambique, health care providers say cancellation or redirection of U.S. PEPFAR funding under the Trump administration have already endangered vulnerable people and cost lives.

(Image credit: Gulshan Khan for NPR)
item.dc:creator: Juana Summers

item.title: A park famed for rare gorillas gears up to fight Ebola and protect its primates
item.description: In Virunga National Park, rangers are on the front lines — playing a critical role to contain the surging virus while coping with an upsurge in conflict-related violence.
item.content:encoded:
In Virunga National Park, rangers are on the front lines — playing a critical role to contain the surging virus while coping with an upsurge in conflict-related violence.

(Image credit: Roberto Schmidt/AFP)
item.dc:creator: Gabrielle Emanuel

item.title: CDC report: Ebola outbreak could rival the worst on record unless world acts
item.description: New modeling from the CDC shows that if measures aren't taken immediately, this outbreak could sicken more than 20,000 people in the next three months.
item.content:encoded:
New modeling from the CDC shows that if measures aren't taken immediately, this outbreak could sicken more than 20,000 people in the next three months.

(Image credit: Daniel Buuma)
item.dc:creator: Rob Stein

item.title: South Africa rolls out game-changing HIV shot amid funding shortfalls
item.description: A new twice-yearly HIV prevention injection could transform South Africa's fight against the epidemic — but U.S. aid cuts and limited doses threaten to slow its impact.
item.content:encoded:
A new twice-yearly HIV prevention injection could transform South Africa's fight against the epidemic — but U.S. aid cuts and limited doses threaten to slow its impact.

(Image credit: Ihsaan Haffe)
item.dc:creator: Kate Bartlett
